title: Normative sociology and normative behaviorism: recent discussions of the relation between empirical social science and normative political theory
author: Lægaard

opening_move.description: The paper opens by diagnosing a 'methodological moment' in political theory: while the strict separation of facts and values has been widely challenged, the precise implications of different alternatives for what political theorists should actually do remain underexplored.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Different views on the fact-value relation have concrete implications for what political theorists should do and which methods they should employ

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper conducts a systematic methodological comparison of two recent approaches without advocating for either; its own contribution is the analytical framework of the four comparison dimensions.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: conceptual analysis and systematic comparison of methodological positions
epistemic_mode.notes: The paper discusses empirical evidence as an object of methodological analysis but does not itself use any empirical data; it is a meta-methodological argument conducted entirely within conceptual/normative space.

confidence_notes: Unusual structure: both interlocutors (Floyd and Modood) are simultaneously 'central' and 'engaged_critically' since the paper subjects both to immanent critique without endorsing either. The paper's own epistemic mode is fully normative/conceptual despite its topic being about the empirical-normative interface.
